Adventure #5—Lights… Camera… Change the Action

Adventure #5—Lights... Camera...
Change the Action
A few days later, when I arrived at the bookstore, I found Tom already there, sipping coffee in the café and jottingAdventure #6—The Write Stuff
down notes on a small piece of paper.
Adventure #7—Oh, What a Tangled
Web We Weave Previous Lesson Next Lesson	Mark as Complete
“So,” said Tom, the moment I sat down. “Did you find any magazines that fit the bill?”	Adventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance
to Come Up with Great Ideas
Adventure #9—The Beginning “Yes,” I said, “I landed on a magazine about horses.”
Postscript... Four Years Later
“And what did you learn from it?”
“Well, let me just say that I have zero interest in horses. So, at first, I couldn’t see what I could possibly apply from
all the articles about curing horse ailments, buying horses, stabling horses and breeding horses.
But then, I saw an advertisement from a horse farm that really stood out, because it listed all the reasons why you
should board your horse with them. They went into great detail about how they take care of the horses, the type
of accommodations, even the type and quality of bedding they use. It was so captivating that I found myself
wishing I had my own horse, just so I could have them look after it.
“And that made me think that, at our bookstore, we never tell people why they should shop with us rather than at
one of the big chains—or at one of the other independent bookstores in town. So, I made a list of the top ten
reasons why someone should come here to buy their books—from the personal, expert service you get from
clerks who really know and love books, to free refills on coffee—and we’re going to post it in the store and on
our website and use it in one way or another in all our advertising.”
I passed Tom a copy of the list. “Very impressive,” he commented, scanning it over. “You’ve really extracted all
the reasons that make your store a unique place for people to buy books. Shows real horse sense.”
“I’ll overlook that horsing around,” I said, “in exchange for a few more of those boxes.”
“I was just thinking,” said Tom, looking around the store and seeming to ignore my request, “what an interesting
thing a business is. It’s so much like a machine with all the parts and processes and interactions: stocking,
ordering, staffing, advertising and selling. And, while I was waiting for you, I’ve been sitting here watching the
gears turn—people come in, look for books, find them or don’t, pay the cashier and head out the door.”
“Actually, sometimes they don’t pay the cashier,” I interrupted. “They take us up on our new offer to keep the book
for a few days before we charge their card.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 9 --

“And how’s that been working out?” Tom leaned forward in his seat, showing genuine interest.
“Great! Nobody’s tried to cheat us. In fact, everyone’s gone out of their way to be fair. I can definitely see where it
has increased sales and profits. Just like encouraging people to call us before they come in and tell us what
books they’re looking for—I could immediately see a boost in our average sale per customer.”
“Glad to hear it,” said Tom. “Anyway, as I sat here watching all the actions that make a bookstore a bookstore, I
thought this would be a good setting to look at boxes that have to do with changing the action.
“In these boxes, instead of looking at the parts and how we might change them, we look at the action itself. For
example, I call the first action box Switch Gears . In this box, you change the speed or quantity of the action. You
speed it up, slow it down, do more. Or my personal favorite: do less.
“‘Speeding up’ is interesting, because many things that won’t work at one speed will work at a different speed.
Like waterskiing. Go slow and you sink. But go faster, and something almost magical happens: Water builds up
under the skis and the pressure keeps you elevated. It’s the same physics that keeps an airplane in the sky, of
course.
And remember how many great ideas you came up with when I pushed you to rapidly write as many as you
could in a set amount of time?”
I nodded with pride. “I sure do. The speed definitely pushed me beyond what I thought possible.”
“Should we try it again?” proposed Tom. “Why don’t we see how many ideas you can come up with by Switching
Gears over the course of five minutes? This time, I’ll scribble down the ideas on this notepad as you come up
with them.”
“Let’s see,” I began, trying to get the creative equivalent of a running start. Tom set the timer on his phone. “What
could I speed up?”
I tapped my fingers on the coffee table in front of me while I looked up and noticed a line forming around the
cashier counter.
“I know—I could speed up the time it takes people to pay for their purchases. People hate to wait in line. That
would give me another advantage I could promote over the chains. I could also speed up the amount of time it
takes for people to receive books that they special order with express delivery. When people want a book, they
don’t want to wait, so the faster the better. I mean, I’ll struggle to compete with the likes of Amazon on speed, but
I can certainly expedite the process.”
“Good! And what could you slow down?” Tom prodded.
“Well… I recently read a study that showed that the longer people spend in a bookstore, the more they buy. I
could slow them down with more chairs and tables and install fiber-optic WiFi that works at lightning speed from
anywhere in the store.
“Now, how can I do more?” I glanced at the timer. I still had two minutes. “More ways to stock more books…
More ways to find out about books… Maybe a website or app where they can look up books themselves. And
more books in the store would mean more sales. I could do that by adding more bookcases and by using some
of the back rooms for special displays.
“Less,” pushed Tom, who’d been busily jotting down my ideas on his notepad.
“Let’s see… Well, what if I did less? I’m the best salesperson I’ve got, but lately I spend all my time ordering,
hiring and solving problems. What if I let other people handle all that, and I went around helping customers? I
know sales would improve. You know what? I’m gonna do it!”
Tom interrupted my brainstorming just as the timer on his phone timer started dinging. He used his pen to count
the points he’d written down. “Well, that seems like about a dozen. Care to guess what the next action box would
be?”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 9 --

“Let’s see,” I replied, “we’ve slowed it down, sped it up, done more and done less. What else can you do to an
action?”
“You could change the periodicity,” he offered.
“The what ?”
“Sorry. What I mean is, you can adjust an action by changing it from something that happens all the time to
something that only happens at certain intervals. Or vice-versa. Change it from something that happens every
once in a while, to something that happens continuously. I call it the Revise the Rhythm box.
“For example, I recently did some work for a mining company. They kept trying to speed up the drilling by
messing with the drill bit—changing the shape, changing the material it was made of, making it rotate faster and
so on. But I found that—due to the nature of the material they were drilling through—the best way to speed up the
drilling was to change the operation from continuous to intermittent—that is, to drill in pulses, rather than
continuously. Same principle works above ground. Often, doing something in short bursts is more effective than
plugging away at it continuously. And vice-versa.”
“Kind of like my lawn sprinkler,” I suggested. “Instead of constantly flooding my lawn with water, it pulses the
water intermittently.”
And suddenly, I had an idea. “You know, this could be a solution to my messy storeroom problem. I’ve been
looking at it as a ‘continuous’ action—it should be kept clean and neat all the time. But what if I stop trying to
make it continuously neat and tidy? What if, instead, I looked at it as an intermittent action, and twice a day, say
at 11 and 4, we can make it someone’s responsibility to go in and straighten it up?”
“Good,” said Tom. “Now, how about an idea that goes from intermittent to continuous?”
I looked at him inquisitively.
“Take ‘just-in-time delivery,’ for example.” Tom went on. “Instead of taking deliveries periodically and needing
huge warehouses, factories take delivery continuously as they use up their stock.”
“That would really solve a big chunk of my storeroom problems. But I doubt I could ever get my publishers to
agree to a more staggered delivery schedule.”
“Wait!” Tom interrupted. “Don’t be so quick to dismiss an idea as inapplicable just because it seems
challenging. Maybe you can’t get your publishers to agree to something like just-in-time delivery.
But perhaps they would agree to—or you could arrange—something that has some of the elements of just-in-
time delivery, such as a speedier delivery, more deliveries more often, and so on.
“Even if you can’t change a thing about how they deliver, perhaps there are ways you could make your own
operation more ‘just-in-time.’
“How can you better anticipate when you’re about to run out of a book? How can you get the order to the
publisher more quickly? And so on and so on. I bet there’s even some affordable AI tech out there for this?
Anyways, that’s something to look into.
“Again, it’s all in how you ask the question. If the question is: ‘Can I get all my publishers to do just-in-time
delivery like suppliers do for some factories?’ then the answer is ‘probably not’—and idea-making stops. But
let’s say that instead you ask, ‘What about the idea of just-in-time delivery can improve how we order, stock and
get books delivered?’ Then your mind opens to possibility and opportunity.”
“Well, I suppose we are a bit behind the times in many of those areas,” I admitted. “Much as I would like to, I
can’t blame everything on the publishers. Do you think I could take another whack at taking something from the
intermittent-to-continuous box?”
“Whack away,” Tom smiled and nodded.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 9 --

“One of the biggest needs is more training for my team. The more they know about books, the more they can
help customers find what they’re looking for. Or even help them find books that they aren’t looking for but would
really enjoy.
“I know the online sellers have fancy recommendation engines that do this, but we could offer it from a human
perspective—which I think people would appreciate as a unique touch.
“But our training has always been so intermittent—a day or two when they start work and an occasional seminar
after they’ve been with us long enough. What if we made training continuous —like getting together right after
closing every day to discuss the latest books?
“Or, I could write a newsletter for my employees! They could take it home, read the articles, and get excited
about all the new releases, which they could then recommend. That’s something I would really enjoy doing that
would be really useful!”
At that moment, we watched a young woman who had been nursing a cup of coffee at a nearby table, get up to
leave the store. It wasn’t the first time I’d noticed her. She came in frequently, usually on Wednesdays—generally
a slow day for us—and she would occasionally ask my advice on new releases. We seemed to like a lot of the
same books. But she never got more than coffee at the café, which wasn’t—I thought to myself—exactly going to
put my daughter through college. Or even buy her a textbook.
“Just a cup of coffee,” said Tom, shaking his head, as if reading my thoughts. “Not much profit in that, I’d
imagine.”
“No, unfortunately, not nearly as much as on the pastries.”
“I’ve been watching her,” Tom went on, scrunching his eyes. “I could see she wanted a pastry—I noticed her
glancing at the display case every once in a while. But she didn’t seem to want to stand in line. Nor did she dare
to ask the salesperson for one. Maybe it’s a dietary thing. Who knows? In any case, she never got up to get one.
Now, am I right to imagine that if more people also bought a pastry, that would help to fatten up your bottom
line?”
“It sure would!”
“Good. Because I took the liberty of rummaging around in a couple of boxes for ideas to see if we couldn’t
somehow satisfy her sweet tooth, and I came across one in the final action box: D.I.I. ”
“D.I.Y.?” I asked. “‘Do it yourself?’”
“No, D.I. I . Do It Itself . Let the part or system do it itself. Let it fix itself or let it perform some other additional and
useful function.
“For example, there used to be a problem with throwing rice at weddings. All that rice had to be picked up
afterward—and it was a pain in the neck. But then someone asked, ‘How could this rice clean itself up?’ And the
answer was birdseed! By using birdseed instead of rice, birds would flock down after the celebration and clean
up the mess.
“Or, like the clever fellow who, instead of trying to make yet another oven cleaner, decided to let the oven D.I.I. ”
“The self-cleaning oven?”
“Exactly. And instead of a stronger tire?”
“A self-sealing one.”
“And instead of a better way for you to blow up an inflatable mattress?
“A self-inflating one!”
“Now,” said Tom, “back to that woman who left without buying a pastry…”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 9 --

“Let her do it herself? Let her serve herself? But how?”
Tom tilted his head but said nothing, allowing me to work this out on my own.
“I suppose that, instead of having the pastries behind a glass case, we could put them out in the open where
people could just come and serve themselves. I bet if it were that easy to get a pastry, many more people would
buy one with their coffee. The payment part would be easy to figure out. We could put a slip of paper under each
pastry plate that customers could take to a cashier at their convenience, or they could pay for it when they pay for
their books.”
“You may be onto something. Test your hypothesis. Try it out. Do an experiment.”
“I will.”
“By the way,” added Tom, somewhat reluctantly, “do you know her?”
“Who? That woman? Not really. Well, yes, sort of. Why do you ask?”
“I don’t mean to be nosy—we inventors are just naturally inquisitive. I was just wondering, because of your
reaction when I pointed her out.”
“You don’t miss much, do you?” I smiled and chuckled. “Her name is Donna. We’ve had a few brief
conversations, but that’s it. I’d like to get to know her better, but I haven’t dated much since my wife died, and I
guess I feel a bit awkward about approaching a customer like that.”
“So, you’re the owner and that’s a negative, right?” Tom asked slowly.
“Yes, you could say that. Hey, I see what you’re getting at: The Make It a Benefit box, right?”
Tom nodded, a huge grin taking over his face. “That’s right! Turn any negative into a positive.”
“Well, what if I just go up to her and be honest and just tell her my dilemma. It would certainly be a way to start a
conversation, anyway.”
“Might work,” agreed Tom, “especially if you buy her that pastry.”
Summary & Exercises
In a Nutshell
Action makes the world go around.
And looking at, thinking about, and changing the action is one of the most powerful ways to come up with ideas.
But before we play in the exercise sandbox, I just want to say…
Wow––you’re halfway through!
I want to tell you again how rare your persistence is. And remind you of how essential persistence is for
creativity.
It’s the quality you’ll consistently find true of virtually every truly creative person: They don’t give up. They try and
they try until they come up with an idea or the answer to a problem.
One has to wonder: Do they come up with the idea or the answer because they’re so creative—or because
they’re so persistent?
Thomas Edison invented the first practical light bulb. But is that because he was a better inventor than his
contemporaries? Or because he alone had the stick-to-it-ness to keep trying after thousands of failures?!
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 9 --

I recently had the chance to prove the power of persistence for myself.
I was reading a little book about the beauty of some classical mathematical proofs that was written for non-
mathematicians. It delved into math facts, like how the angles of a triangle always add up to 180 degrees and
that there’s an infinite number of prime numbers.
But because it was a small book, it lacked detailed explanations. There is much that the author assumes the
reader knows or understands. And it wasn’t long before I was absolutely stumped by one of the proofs. I just
couldn’t figure out why it proved what it was supposed to prove.
So, every day for a few days, I re-read that section of the book and tried to figure it out anew. I knew it would
eventually break through. And, sure enough, after a few days, I suddenly understood it.
I didn’t get any smarter during that time. I received no new information or assistance. It was simply a result of
nothing but pure persistence.
There’s an important aspect of persistence that came up in this Adventure—to persist with ideas that may seem
impossible. Because they may not actually be impossible. Or you may be able to modify an idea in such a way
that it actually becomes possible.
Far too often, people just give up on an idea that seems difficult to implement. But with slight modification, it can
often be a perfectly workable solution to the problem.
Another thing about challenging ideas––they may inspire or make you think of a solution that may not have come
to you otherwise.
When you come up against an idea that you’re ready to dismiss as too difficult, ask yourself three questions first:
1. “Is there anything I can do to this idea (or the situation) to make it workable?” (Are you sure you’ve explored all
possible ways?)
2. “Can I use this idea for anything––or any where ––else? Don’t let good ideas go to waste. A well-known
advertising copywriter used to come to meetings with a big box filled with index cards. When ideas were
needed, he would open the box and flip through his collection of ideas and headlines that had previously been
rejected as unusable by clients or weren’t what was needed at the time. Often, this process allowed him to find
exactly what was needed.)
3. “What else does this difficult idea make me think of?” Sometimes a random association will trigger a more
practical one. Sometimes, it may even trigger exactly the right idea!
And now, let’s play in the Action boxes—perhaps the most exciting boxes in this system…
Click here to download this fillable PDF
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 9 --

-- 8 of 9 --

Your Action Steps
1. Think back. Is there a problem you’ve given up trying to solve—at home or at the office? Whether the problem	MODULES
is major or minor, go back and solve it. Don’t give up until you come up with a workable solution. Practice
persistence! Develop that muscle!
2. Everything in life has its own speed and its own rhythm. Become more aware of it. See what you can find that
might need changing. A good place to start might be your own working speed. Most people work too slowly.
Most businesses move, react and change far too slowly (as Joe Vitale points out in his book entitled Money
Loves Speed ). So, increase the pace and see what happens. This gives you a huge advantage! At the same
time, look for balance. What is being short-changed by being rushed, and how might it benefit from a slower
pace?
Next up: We’ll see how to apply Idea Power techniques to one of my favorite subjects: writing!
When you want to come up with ideas, don’t ask yourself questions that stop idea generation
(such as questions that can be answered with a “no”). Instead, ask positive questions that allow
for possibilities.
All of these resources are also available in your Idea Power Mindbook if you prefer to write
them out by hand.
